 {"id":"bd-1uk","title":"Connector: process/env/path/crypto/time minimal APIs","description":"# Goal
Fill remaining minimal OS surface in PiJS without pulling in Node/Bun.

# Scope / Deliverables
- `pi.env.get` (whitelisted keys only).
- `pi.process.cwd`, `pi.process.args` (read-only).
- `pi.path` helpers (join, basename, normalize).
- `pi.crypto` subset (hashing, random bytes) backed by Rust.
- `pi.time` (monotonic now, sleep via timers).
- Structured logs for env access and crypto ops (non-secret metadata only).

# Security Invariants
- No ability to read arbitrary env vars.
- Randomness and hashing are constant-time and auditable.

- Unit tests for env allowlist enforcement.

- E2E security suite validates env denial.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T17:22:47.943408211Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T18:07:24.845953399Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1uk","depends_on_id":"bd-37z","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T17:24:57.190277129Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1uk","depends_on_id":"bd-h04","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T17:24:57.652329703Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-1vo","title":"Migrate OpenAI provider to asupersync HTTP","description":"# Migrate OpenAI provider to asupersync HTTP

## Goal
Replace reqwest usage in src/providers/openai.rs with asupersync HTTP client.

## Background
OpenAI provider handles:
- Chat completions API
- Function calling (tools)
- SSE streaming with data: [DONE] termination
- 645 lines, 3 unit tests

## Implementation
Same pattern as Anthropic migration:
1. Replace reqwest::Client with crate::http::Client
2. Replace request building with asupersync RequestBuilder
3. Replace SSE streaming with SseStream adapter
4. Add Cx parameter to async functions

## OpenAI-Specific Considerations
1. **SSE Format**: Uses "data: [DONE]" as termination marker
2. **Tool Calls**: JSON in function_call field
3. **Streaming Deltas**: content delta in choices[0].delta.content
4. **Error Format**: Different error JSON structure from Anthropic

## Dependencies
- bd-9sa (HTTP client wrapper)
- bd-pwz (SSE streaming adapter)
- bd-37l (Anthropic migration first - establishes pattern)

## Testing
- 3 existing unit tests must pass
- Integration test with mock server

## Files
- src/providers/openai.rs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] No reqwest imports
- [ ] All tests pass
- [ ] Streaming works
- [ ] Tool calls work","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:32:59.929871005Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T03:33:41.150888038Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1vo","depends_on_id":"bd-37l","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:33:41.150863292Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1vo","depends_on_id":"bd-gi3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:32:59.939779889Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1vo","depends_on_id":"bd-pwz","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:33:40.889506248Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

+{"id":"bd-2hr","title":"Spec: Unified JS+WASM capability model","description":"# Goal
Define a **single, coherent capability model** that applies equally to JS (PiJS) and WASM extensions, so security policy, logs, and tooling are identical across runtimes.

# Why
- Avoid divergent permission semantics between JS and WASM.
- Enable shared tooling (manifests, scanners, audit logs) and a consistent user mental model.

# Scope / Deliverables
- Capability taxonomy: common names, scopes, and semantics across JS/WASM.
- Hostcall mapping: how JS `pi.*` calls and WASM hostcalls map to the same capabilities.
- Manifest alignment: how a single manifest expresses capabilities for both runtimes.
- Logging schema alignment: identical fields and redaction rules.

# Constraints
- Must compose with `bd-37z` ABI.
- Must be compatible with WASM host (`bd-nom`) and JS runtime (`bd-123`).

# Tests This Enables
- Cross-runtime capability conformance tests.
- E2E security suite verifying identical enforcement paths.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:09:44.048778878Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T18:09:52.819810195Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2hr","depends_on_id":"bd-123","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:09:52.819769328Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2hr","depends_on_id":"bd-37z","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:09:52.320774486Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2jh","title":"Implement error handling path tests","description":"# Implement error handling path tests

## Goal
Test all error conditions that can occur during normal operation, with detailed logging.

## Background
Error paths are currently untested but critical for UX:
- Network failures
- API rate limits
- Authentication failures
- Malformed responses
- File system errors
- Timeout scenarios

## Runtime + Determinism
- Do not introduce new tokio-only tests; use `#[asupersync::test]` or a runtime wrapper.
- All API error cases must use VCR playback (no live network).
- File system errors must use temp dirs and deterministic paths.

## Logging Requirements
- Use TestLogger (bd-3ml).
- Log cassette path, error category, and sanitized error details.
- On failure, dump expected vs actual error variants + any response body fragments.

## Test Categories

### Network Errors
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_connection_refused() {
    let vcr = VcrRecorder::new("network_connection_refused");
    let provider = AnthropicProvider::new_with_client(vcr.client());

    let result = provider.stream(&context, &options).await;
    assert!(matches!(result, Err(Error::Network { .. })));
}
```

### API Errors
- 429 rate limit (retry-after)
- 401 auth failure
- 403 forbidden
- 400 bad request
- 500 server error
- 529 overloaded

### Malformed Response Errors
- Invalid JSON body

 {"id":"bd-9sa","title":"Implement asupersync HTTP client wrapper","description":"# Implement asupersync HTTP client wrapper

## Goal
Create a functional HTTP client in src/http/client.rs using asupersync APIs.

## Current State
src/http/client.rs has:
- Client struct (empty)
- RequestBuilder struct with method/url/headers/body
- RequestBuilder::send() returns Err("asupersync HTTP client not yet implemented")
- RequestBuilder::json() silently ignores serialization errors

## Implementation Requirements

### Client API
```rust
use asupersync::{Cx, http::client::HttpClient};
use asupersync::tls::TlsConnectorBuilder;

pub struct Client {
    inner: HttpClient,
}

impl Client {
    pub fn new() -> Result<Self, Error> {
        // Create TLS connector with native roots
        let tls = TlsConnectorBuilder::new()
            .with_native_roots()?
            .build()?;
        let inner = HttpClient::new(tls);
        Ok(Self { inner })
    }
    
    pub fn request(&self, method: Method, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder {
        RequestBuilder::new(self, method, url)
    }
    
    pub fn get(&self, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder { ... }
    pub fn post(&self, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder { ... }
}
```

### RequestBuilder API
```rust
impl RequestBuilder {
    pub fn header(mut self, key: &str, value: &str) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn json<T: Serialize>(mut self, body: &T) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn body(mut self, bytes: Vec<u8>) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn timeout(mut self, duration: Duration) -> Self { ... }
    
    pub async fn send(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<Response, Error> {
        // Actually send the request using asupersync
    }
}
```

### Response API
```rust
pub struct Response {
    status: StatusCode,
    headers: Headers,
    body: BodyReader,  // Async streaming body
}

impl Response {
    pub fn status(&self) -> StatusCode { ... }
    pub fn headers(&self) -> &Headers { ... }
    pub async fn text(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<String, Error> { ... }
    pub async fn json<T: DeserializeOwned>(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<T, Error> { ... }
    pub fn bytes_stream(self) -> impl Stream<Item = Result<Bytes, Error>> { ... }
}
```

## Key Considerations
1. **TLS**: Use rustls with native root certificates (no openssl)
2. **Timeout**: Support per-request timeout via asupersync Budget
3. **Streaming**: Response body must support async streaming for SSE
4. **Headers**: Support standard headers (Content-Type, Authorization, etc.)
5. **Error handling**: Wrap asupersync errors in pi's Error type

## Testing
- Unit test: Client creation succeeds
- Unit test: RequestBuilder builds correct request
- Integration test: GET request to httpbin.org/get
- Integration test: POST with JSON body
- Integration test: Timeout behavior

 {"id":"bd-ah1","title":"Implement session persistence cycle tests","description":"# Implement session persistence cycle tests

## Goal
Test the full session lifecycle: create → save → reload → verify integrity, with detailed logs.

## Background
Session persistence involves:
- JSONL file format (one JSON object per line)
- Header with metadata (version, cwd, timestamp)
- Message entries with parent/child relationships
- Tree structure for conversation branching
- File locking for concurrent access

Current tests cover serialization but not full I/O cycles.

## Runtime + Determinism
- Do not introduce new tokio-only tests; use `#[asupersync::test]` or a runtime wrapper.
- Use temp dirs and deterministic paths; avoid asserting exact timestamps.

## Logging Requirements
- Use TestLogger (bd-3ml).
- Log session path, header fields, message IDs, tree shape, and file sizes.
- On failure, dump the JSONL contents (redacted) and parsed header.

## Test Categories

### 1. Basic Persistence Cycle Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_session_save_and_reload() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let mut session = Session::new("/project/path");
    session.add_user_message("Hello");
    session.add_assistant_message("Hi there!");

    session.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let loaded = Session::load(&session_path).await.unwrap();
    assert_eq!(loaded.messages().len(), 2);
}
```

### 2. Tree Structure Persistence Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_branching_persists() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let mut session = Session::new("/project");
    let root_id = session.add_user_message("Root");
    session.add_assistant_message("Branch A");

    session.navigate_to(root_id.clone());
    session.add_assistant_message("Branch B");

    session.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let loaded = Session::load(&session_path).await.unwrap();
    let children = loaded.children_of(&root_id);
    assert_eq!(children.len(), 2);
}
```

### 3. Header Metadata Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_header_metadata_preserved() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let mut session = Session::new("/my/project/path");
    session.set_provider("anthropic");
    session.set_model("claude-opus-4");
    session.set_thinking_level(ThinkingLevel::High);
    session.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let loaded = Session::load(&session_path).await.unwrap();
    assert_eq!(loaded.cwd(), "/my/project/path");
}
```

### 4. Entry Type Persistence Tests
- ModelChange entries
- ThinkingLevel entries
- Compaction entries
- Tool invocation entries (if serialized)

### 5. Error Handling Tests
- Missing file
- Corrupted JSONL
- Partial corruption (valid header + bad line)

### 6. Concurrent Access Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_concurrent_writes_locked() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let session1 = Session::new("/project");
    session1.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let path = session_path.clone();
    let handle1 = spawn(async move {
        let mut s = Session::load(&path).await.unwrap();
        s.add_user_message("From task 1");
        s.persist(&path).await
    });

    let path = session_path.clone();
    let handle2 = spawn(async move {
        let mut s = Session::load(&path).await.unwrap();
        s.add_user_message("From task 2");
        s.persist(&path).await
    });

    let (r1, r2) = join(handle1, handle2);
    assert!(r1.unwrap().is_ok() || r2.unwrap().is_ok());
}
```

### 7. Directory Structure Tests
- Persist creates nested directories when needed

None (uses existing session module)

-{"id":"bd-btq","title":"Extensions: Node/Bun-free runtime (PiJS) — prove superior path","description":"# Goal
Deliver a Node/Bun-free extension runtime (PiJS) that is **more secure, more deterministic, and faster** than legacy Node/Bun extensions **without losing compatibility** for representative real-world extensions.

# Background / Motivation
- Mario’s critique is correct: QuickJS lacks Node/Bun OS wrappers and event loop. We replace that with a **minimal, capability-gated PiJS runtime + deterministic event loop**.
- We want a system that feels *better* to extension authors: clearer errors, explicit permissions, and reproducible behavior.

# Strategy (PiJS)
1) **Connector-first architecture** — all I/O is explicit `host.call` into the connector dispatcher; no ambient OS APIs.
2) **Minimal JS stdlib** — `pi.fs`, `pi.http`, `pi.timers`, `pi.path`, `pi.env`, `pi.crypto`, `pi.time` mapped to connectors.
3) **Deterministic event loop** — microtasks, timers, hostcall promises driven by asupersync with strict ordering rules.
4) **Compatibility layer** — extc rewrite + shims; unsupported APIs rejected with actionable diagnostics.
5) **Security + performance proof** — capability gating, resource limits, benchmarks with reproducible outputs.
6) **Developer UX** — structured logs + trace viewer + remediation hints.
7) **Testing** — comprehensive unit, conformance, and E2E scripts with detailed logging artifacts.

# Success Criteria
- Representative JS extensions run without Node/Bun.
- Deterministic ordering across repeated runs (trace equality).
- Capability policy enforced for every hostcall with structured logs.
- Benchmarks meet or beat budgets.
- Evidence binder (docs + logs + benchmarks) is reproducible.

# Non-Goals
- Full Node/Bun parity.
- Implicit global OS access.

# Notes for Future Self

+{"id":"bd-btq","title":"Extensions: Node/Bun-free runtime (PiJS) — prove superior path","description":"# Goal
Deliver a Node/Bun-free extension runtime (PiJS) that is **more secure, more deterministic, and faster** than legacy Node/Bun extensions **without losing compatibility** for representative real-world extensions.

# Background / Motivation
- Mario’s critique is correct: QuickJS lacks Node/Bun OS wrappers and event loop. We replace that with a **minimal, capability-gated PiJS runtime + deterministic event loop**.
- We want a system that feels *better* to extension authors: clearer errors, explicit permissions, and reproducible behavior.

# Strategy (PiJS)
1) **Connector-first architecture** — all I/O is explicit `host.call` into the connector dispatcher; no ambient OS APIs.
2) **Minimal JS stdlib** — `pi.fs`, `pi.http`, `pi.timers`, `pi.path`, `pi.env`, `pi.crypto`, `pi.time` mapped to connectors.
3) **Deterministic event loop** — microtasks, timers, hostcall promises driven by asupersync with strict ordering rules.
4) **Compatibility layer** — extc rewrite + shims; unsupported APIs rejected with actionable diagnostics.
5) **Security + performance proof** — capability gating, resource limits, benchmarks with reproducible outputs.
6) **Developer UX** — structured logs + trace viewer + remediation hints.
7) **Testing** — comprehensive unit, conformance, and E2E scripts with detailed logging artifacts.

# Success Criteria
- Representative JS extensions run without Node/Bun.
- Deterministic ordering across repeated runs (trace equality).
- Capability policy enforced for every hostcall with structured logs.
- Benchmarks meet or beat budgets.
- Evidence binder (docs + logs + benchmarks) is reproducible.

# Non-Goals
- Full Node/Bun parity.
- Implicit global OS access.

# Notes for Future Self

 {"id":"bd-pwz","title":"Implement SSE streaming adapter for asupersync","description":"# Implement SSE streaming adapter for asupersync

## Goal
Bridge asupersync HTTP streaming response to the existing SSE parser (src/sse.rs).

## Background
The SSE parser in src/sse.rs is already complete and well-tested:
- SseParser::new() creates parser
- SseParser::feed(&str) returns Vec<SseEvent>
- Handles: event types, data fields, IDs, comments, multiline data
- 9 unit tests covering all edge cases

The challenge is connecting asupersync's streaming response body to this parser.

## Implementation

### Streaming Adapter
```rust
// src/http/sse.rs (new file or extend existing)

use crate::sse::{SseParser, SseEvent};
use asupersync::Cx;

pub struct SseStream {
    parser: SseParser,
    body: BodyReader,
    buffer: String,
}

impl SseStream {
    pub fn new(response: Response) -> Self {
        Self {
            parser: SseParser::new(),
            body: response.into_body(),
            buffer: String::new(),
        }
    }
    
    pub async fn next(&mut self, cx: &Cx) -> Option<Result<SseEvent, Error>> {
        loop {
            // Try to parse buffered data first
            let events = self.parser.feed(&self.buffer);
            if !events.is_empty() {
                self.buffer.clear();
                return Some(Ok(events.remove(0)));
            }
            
            // Read more data from body stream
            match self.body.read_chunk(cx).await {
                Ok(Some(chunk)) => {
                    self.buffer.push_str(&String::from_utf8_lossy(&chunk));
                }
                Ok(None) => return None,  // Stream ended
                Err(e) => return Some(Err(e.into())),
            }
        }
    }
}

// Or implement as a proper Stream trait for use with async iteration
impl Stream for SseStream {
    type Item = Result<SseEvent, Error>;
    
    fn poll_next(self: Pin<&mut Self>, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Option<Self::Item>> {
        // ...
    }
}
```

### Usage Pattern (in providers)
```rust
// In anthropic.rs
let response = client.post(url).json(&request).send(cx).await?;
let mut sse = SseStream::new(response);

while let Some(event) = sse.next(cx).await {
    match event? {
        SseEvent { data, event_type, .. } => {
            // Process Anthropic streaming event
        }
    }
}
```

## Considerations
1. **Backpressure**: Don't buffer entire response; process incrementally
2. **Error recovery**: Handle malformed events gracefully (log and continue)
3. **Cancellation**: Support clean shutdown via Cx cancellation
4. **Memory**: Limit buffer size to prevent OOM on malicious servers

## Dependencies
- bd-9sa (HTTP client wrapper must exist first)

## Testing
- Unit test: SseStream parses single event
- Unit test: SseStream handles multiline data
- Unit test: SseStream handles stream interruption
- Integration test: Stream from mock HTTP server

### Create Themed Styles

```rust
// src/interactive.rs

fn create_styles(theme: &Theme) -> Styles {
    Styles {
        message_user: lipgloss::Style::new()
            .foreground(lipgloss::Color::hex(&theme.colors.accent)),
        
        message_assistant: lipgloss::Style::new()
            .foreground(lipgloss::Color::hex(&theme.colors.foreground)),
        
        thinking: lipgloss::Style::new()
            .foreground(lipgloss::Color::hex(&theme.colors.muted))
            .italic(true),
        
        error: lipgloss::Style::new()
            .foreground(lipgloss::Color::hex(&theme.colors.error))
            .bold(true),
        
        success: lipgloss::Style::new()
            .foreground(lipgloss::Color::hex(&theme.colors.success)),
        
        border: lipgloss::Style::new()
            .border_foreground(lipgloss::Color::hex(&theme.ui.border)),
        
        // ... more styles
    }
}
```

### Apply to PiApp
```rust
impl PiApp {
    pub fn new(config: Config) -> Self {
        let theme = config.theme()
            .map(|name| Theme::load_by_name(&name).ok())
            .flatten()
            .unwrap_or_else(Theme::dark);
        
        let styles = create_styles(&theme);
        
        Self {
            theme,
            styles,
            // ...
        }
    }
}
```

### Apply to Glamour (Markdown)
```rust
fn create_glamour_style(theme: &Theme) -> glamour::Style {
    glamour::Style {
        document: glamour::StyleBlock {
            color: Some(theme.colors.foreground.clone()),
            background_color: Some(theme.colors.background.clone()),
            ..Default::default()
        },
        heading: glamour::StyleBlock {
            color: Some(theme.colors.accent.clone()),
            bold: Some(true),
            ..Default::default()
        },
        code: glamour::StyleBlock {
            color: Some(theme.syntax.string.clone()),
            ..Default::default()
        },
        // ... more elements
    }
}
```
